coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of NiFe2O4 integrated with EDTA-derived carbon dots for enhanced removal of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Fangbing
Zhang, Wentao
Chen, Wenjie
Wang, Jing
Yang, Qingfeng
Zhu, Wenxin
Wang, Jianlong
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon-based nanomaterials especially carbon dots (CDs) show as the promising candidates for organic contaminant removal because of their abundant functional groups, easy-of-preparation and high surface area. However, the difficulties in separation and recycle of CDs arising from its high water solubility and low molecular weight limit its application in this area. Herein, we demonstrate a one-pot solvothermal method for fabricating NiFe2O4-carbon dot (NiFeC) nanocomposites, which solve its limitations and mitigate the usual fabrication drawbacks by self-assembly such as relatively weak interaction, time-consuming and lack of recycling potential. Series characterizations confirmed the successful fabrication of NiFeC nanocomposites with great uniformity in morphology and exhibited excellent dispersibility in water. The adsorption behavior of the as-prepared nanocomposites was systematically studied using kinetic and equilibrium experiments. Owing to abundant hydroxy and carboxyl groups introduced from CDs, NiFeC nanocomposites offered significantly enhanced tetracycline (TC) removal ability with the maximum adsorption capacity of 591.72mgg−1, as well as the excellent regenerability. Moreover, XPS and FT-IR analysis revealed the adsorption mechanism was attributed to surface complexation, cation exchange and electrostatic interaction between TC and the NiFeC. With the higher adsorption capacity and simpler synthesis method than traditional adsorbents, NiFeC nanocomposites have promising potential for the removal of TC from wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Adsorption
Tetracycline
Solvothermal

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financed by Grants from the New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-13-0483), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31371813, No. 31201357), the Shaanxi Provincial Research Fund (2014KJXX-42, 2014K02-13-03, 2014K13-10), Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University of China (2014YB093, 2452015257).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image (magnification 13,000×) of NiFeC nanocomposites; (b) TEM image (magnification 100,000×) of NiFeC nanocomposites. (c) SEM image (magnification 50,000×) of high concentration NiFeC and EDAX element composition analysis of NiFeC (top right corner inset). (d–f) EDX elemental mappings of C, Fe and Ni, respectively. Magnification: 50,000×.

(a) The XRD patterns of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles and NiFeC nanocomposites; (b) magnetization curves of NiFe2O4 and NiFeC, the left inset is magnified hysteresis loops from −100 to 100Oe and the right inset shows the magnetic separation of NiFe2O4 and NiFeC.

(a) Effect of pH values on the NiFeC of TC adsorption. (b) Adsorption capacities of TC from an aqueous solution over different adsorbents including NiFe2O4 and NiFeC. Experimental conditions: adsorbent dosage 80mgL−1, initial TC concentration: 100mgL−1, pH=8.0±0.05.

(a) Pseudo-second-order kinetic model and (b) intra-particle diffusion kinetic model of TC on NiFeC nanocomposites. (c) Adsorption Langmuir isotherm and (d) the linear fit of the model of TC on NiFeC at different temperatures. (e) Adsorption Freundlich isotherm and (f) the linear fit of the model of TC on NiFeC at different temperatures. Experimental conditions: adsorbent dose 80mgL−1, initial TC concentration: 100mgL−1, pH=8.0±0.05.
Effect of interfering ions on the removal efficiency of TC on NiFeC nanocomposites: natrium ions and kalium ions under different concentrations.
FT-IR spectra for TC, NiFeC and TC-loaded NiFeC.
O 1s XPS spectra of NiFeC nanocomposites before (a) and after (b) TC adsorption.
Mechanistic illustration of adsorption of TC on NiFeC nanocomposites.
Langmuir and Freundlich parameters of NiFeC for TC adsorption.
Comparison of maximum adsorption for TC on various adsorbents reported in literatures.
Binding energy and relative content of O in NiFeC nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of NiFe2O4 integrated with EDTA-derived carbon dots for enhanced removal of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hybrid Rapeseed Research Center of Shaanxi Provice (SXHRRC), Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hybrid Rapeseed Research Center of Shaanxi Provice (SXHRRC)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon-based nanomaterials especially carbon dots (CDs) show as the promising candidates for organic contaminant removal because of their abundant functional groups, easy-of-preparation and high surface area. However, the difficulties in separation and recycle of CDs arising from its high water solubility and low molecular weight limit its application in this area. Herein, we demonstrate a one-pot solvothermal method for fabricating NiFe2O4-carbon dot (NiFeC) nanocomposites, which solve its limitations and mitigate the usual fabrication drawbacks by self-assembly such as relatively weak interaction, time-consuming and lack of recycling potential. Series characterizations confirmed the successful fabrication of NiFeC nanocomposites with great uniformity in morphology and exhibited excellent dispersibility in water. The adsorption behavior of the as-prepared nanocomposites was systematically studied using kinetic and equilibrium experiments. Owing to abundant hydroxy and carboxyl groups introduced from CDs, NiFeC nanocomposites offered significantly enhanced tetracycline (TC) removal ability with the maximum adsorption capacity of 591.72mgg−1, as well as the excellent regenerability. Moreover, XPS and FT-IR analysis revealed the adsorption mechanism was attributed to surface complexation, cation exchange and electrostatic interaction between TC and the NiFeC. With the higher adsorption capacity and simpler synthesis method than traditional adsorbents, NiFeC nanocomposites have promising potential for the removal of TC from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical antibiotics, extensively used in human disease treatment and livestock production, however, have been regarded as a class of worldwide pollutants due to their ineffective biological degradation [1]. With the widespread use and often misuse of conventional antibiotics, increasing occurrence of antibiotic resistance occurs globally among various pathogens [2,3]. Especially, tetracycline, a group of commonly used antibiotics, is concentrated in soil and groundwater and always difficult to completely remove from the environment [4]. To date, various techniques have been developed to remove antibiotics from aqueous solution, including chemical oxidation, catalytic degradation, membrane filtration and adsorption [5–8]. Among these techniques, adsorption is considered to be simple and cost-effective, and remains one of the most attractive methods [9].

Magnetic nanoparticles based nanocomposites have been widely explored to cross-link and remove contaminants from environment, facilitating the safety of drinking water [10,11]. Compared to other currently available adsorbents, magnetic MFe2O4 (M denotes a divalent metal such as NiII, MnII) nanoparticles (NPs) have drawn great attention due to their outstanding properties like high adsorption capacity, easy operation process, environmentally benign nature and large scale production [12,13]. They are capable of affecting the bioavailability of antibiotic by forming stable metal-ligand with antibiotic molecules [14]. However, the drawback of using MFe2O4 as adsorbent is that they lead to aggregation that cannot endow the good dispersity in aqueous solution [15,16]. Over the past few years, MFe2O4 functionalized 2D nanosheets like graphene and MoS2 have made as a complementary composite with each other to solve this problem [17,18]. Unfortunately, the synthetic process of these hybrid composites often requires multistep procedure which is rather tedious and expensive. Moreover, despite remarkable advances made in the diverse preparation and application of metal oxide nanomaterials [19,20], there have been few reports on the functionalization of NiFe2O4 in adsorbents.

Carbon dots (CDs), with numerous oxygen-containing functional groups (OH, COOH and CO) on their surfaces, are particularly encouraging owing to their outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, free of substrate, and water dispersity [21]. CDs offer numerous exciting applications, such as bioimaging, optoelectronic devices and biological labeling [22]. Though prominent aqueous solubility and modifiable ability in aqueous solution, CDs were limited in application as adsorbents, for reasons that the complicated separation process and a lack of recycling potential of CDs. In addition, how to prepare multifunctional CDs-based adsorbents to overcome the tedious, multistep synthetic procedures is still a challenge [23]. Therefore, efficient strategies for fabrication of CDs functionalized adsorbents are still required for further investigation.

In this work, we develop here a one-pot solvothermal method to fabricate a multi-functional CDs-modified NiFe2O4 nanocomposites adsorbent with high adsorption capacity, excellent dispersibility in water. Ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), which was chosen as the precursor of CDs with abundant hydroxy groups, not only provides a mass of bonding sites for antibiotics molecules but also reduces magnetic nanoparticles agglomeration because of the protection effect of CDs simultaneously deposited on the surface of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles via solvothermal treatment [24]. The prepared adsorbent can intelligently regulate adsorption capacity by changing the EDTA-2Na concentrations. Moreover, the magnetic ferrite is able to provide good recycling potential as well as enhance the adsorption performance of nanocomposites through binding to antibiotics [25]. As a widely used broad-spectrum antibiotic, TC is thus selected to examine the adsorption performance of NiFeC nanocomposites. In detail, the underlying adsorption mechanism was explored by the pH effect of the adsorption process, as well as with the aid of a series of structural characterizations, such as FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade and purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Milli-Q element ultrapure water (18.2Ω, Millipore, Massachusetts) was used to prepare all solutions. All materials were used as-received without further treatment.

Magnetic NiFeC nanocomposites were prepared by a modified solvothermal method. In brief, 0.12g NiCl2·6H2O, 0.27g FeCl3·6H2O and 0.5g NaAc were dissolved in 20mL of ethylene glycol under bath sonication for 20min; when a clear solution was obtained, EDTA-2Na was introduced subsequently to the mixture. The mixing solution of EDTA-2Na concentration was optimized in the preliminary experiments (Table S1 and Fig. S1), and EDTA-2Na was added to a final concentration of 4mgmL−1. Then after sonication for 10min, the mixture solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200°C for 10h. The resultant black product was retrieved by a external magnet and washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, then dried at 60°C in a vacuum oven. For comparison, the pure NiFe2O4 was synthesized under the same condition without adding EDTA-2Na.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi S-4800. Transmittance electron microscope (TEM) photographs were examined by a Philips Tecnai F20 (Oregon) or Carlzeiss EM912ΩEF-TEM (Germany). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a powder diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advanced Diffractometer System) with a CuKα (1.5418Å) source, and angular variation of 20–80° operated at a tube voltage of 40kV and a tube current of 40mA. UV–visible spectra were measured using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected using an Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer equipped with an AlKa X-ray source (1486.6eV). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Vetex70 (Bruker Corp, Germany) using KBr pellets at room temperature. The N2 adsorption was measured under nitrogen atmosphere using a Gemini VII2390 instrument. The magnetic properties of the magnetic nanohybrid materials were characterized via vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM, LAKESHORE-7304) at room temperature. The thermal stability and relative Fe content of the composite NFs were investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) with a Netzsch Tartus TG209 F3 Analyzer (Germany). TGA was conducted from 25 to 1000°C with a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 and a constant air flow of 60mLmin−1.

To evaluate the efficiency of NiFeC nanocomposites for TC adsorption in aqueous solution, a series of batch adsorption experiments were studied. The different concentrations of TC solutions were prepared by dilution of the stock solution in distilled water. Bath adsorption experiments were performed in conical flasks by agitating 200mg NiFeC nanocomposites adsorbent with a series of 20mL TC solutions from 25 to 150mgL−1, and then the conical flasks were wrapped with aluminum foil to avoid light exposure and shaken at 130rpm under different temperatures. After the adsorbent were separated by an external magnet, the supernatant of TC solution were monitored via a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at a fixed wavelength of 358nm. To investigate the effect of pH values on adsorption process, the initial pH values of TC solutions (100mgL−1) in the range of 3–11 were adjusted using 0.1M HCl and 0.1M NaOH solutions. The amount of adsorbed TC were measured from the difference between the initial (C0) and equilibrium (Ce) concentrations in the supernatant after separation of the spent adsorbent using an external magnet. The equilibrium adsorption amounts qe (mgg−1) can be calculated by the Eq. (1), where V (L) denotes the volume of TC solutions, and W is the mass in milligram of the NiFeC nanocomposites used. The experiments were conducted in tripartite to ensure the reliability of results.
(1)
q
e
=
(
C
0
-
C
e
) V
m

In the adsorption kinetics experiment, conditions were operated as the same as the equilibrium studies. The 80mgL−1 adsorbent was added in 100mgL−1 TC solution with the initial pH of 8.01±0.05. TC concentrations of all samples were measured, and the amounts of TC adsorbed at specific time were determined by Eq. (2)
(2)
q
e
=
(
C
0
-
C
t
) V
m
Herein, qt (mgg−1) and Ct (mgL−1) are the amounts of adsorbed TC and the concentration of TC at time t, respectively.

Furthermore, to study the interference effect of the other ions, various salts including NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, and CaCl2 with different concentrations were added into the TC solutions in the context of the same treatment with other conditions. For regeneration, the retrieved adsorbent was carried out by washing with distilled water for several times, then mixed with 0.1M NaOH solution and shaken at 130rpm for 1h. The regenerated adsorbent was washed with distilled water until a neutral pH was obtained. The experiments were conducted in tripartite to ensure the reliability of results.

The morphology and structure of NiFe2O4-carbon dots (NiFeC) nanocomposites were elucidated by scanning electron microscopy. Fig.1 a indicates the NiFeC nanoparticles are in nearly uniform size with diameter ranging from 130 to 180nm. From the SEM image of pure NiFe2O4 nanoparticles (Fig. S2), the NiFeC nanocomposites are close to NiFe2O4 nanoparticles in size uniformity after solvothermal treatment with EDTA-2Na. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of NiFeC in Fig.1b clearly shows that the NiFeC nanocomposites have a clear outer shell of single nanoparticle, indicating CDs are integrated to the surface of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles successfully [26]. The results from EDAX analysis further confirms the formation of CDs after the solvothermal treatment (the right inset of Fig.1c), and CDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of NiFe2O4 (Fig.1d–f)

To investigate the magnetic properties of NiFe2O4 and NiFeC, the hysteresis loops were recorded by the magnetometer at room temperature. As shown in Fig.2b, the result indicates that the saturation magnetization (Ms) for NiFe2O4 and NiFeC are 44.9 and 38.7emug−1, respectively, which demonstrates an apparent superparamagnetism of these nanoparticles, thus benefiting for the magnetic separation and recycle [29,30]. What’s more, the reduction of the saturation magnetization value of NiFeC could be owed to the functionalization of CDs onto magnetic NiFe2O4, further confirming the successful synthesis of NiFeC. The right inset of Fig.2b shows that the NiFeC is almost separated completely in the applied magnetic field. Moreover, the specific surface area and porosity of NiFeC were measured by nitrogen gas adsorption technique (Fig. S4). It has a BET surface area of 62.17m2 g−1 and a total pore volume of 0.16cm3 g−1, which are higher than the surface area and total pore volume of NiFe2O4 [31]. Therefore, the NiFeC nanocomposites can provide abundant active sites for TC uptake. The corresponding pore size distribution curve was calculated from the adsorption branch by the Barrett Joyner Halenda (BJH) method (Fig. S4b). The average pore sizes of NiFeC nanocomposites are 4.33nm.

Considering the pH values of TC on the adsorption process as well as the surface properties of NiFeC nanocomposites with the change of pH, it is necessary to study the adsorption performance of NiFeC at different pH values. Here, we measured the adsorption capacity within a pH range from 2 to 10 (shown in Fig.3 a) [25,32]. According to the literature, TC molecules exhibit the aqueous dissociation constants as pK 1 =3.3, pK 2 =7.68, pK 3 =9.69. When the solution pH below 3.3 (pK 1), TC is mainly present in the solution as TCH3 +. With increasing the pH values of solution from 3.3 to 7.68 (pK 2), the predominant tetracycline species are TCH2 and TCH−. At higher pH values ranging from 7.68 to 9.69 (pK 3), TC abundant existing species are TCH− and TC2− [2,33]. In addition, the point of zero charge of the NiFeC was determined using a salt addition method of Mahmood et al. [34]. Based on the point of zero charge pH (pHpzc) characterization (Fig. S5), the pHpzc value of NiFeC nanocomposite was determined as 8.7. As shown in Fig.3a, NiFeC exhibits a low adsorption capability when the pH value below 3.3, which might be owed to the electrostatic repulsion between TCH3 + and NiFeC surface as well as the competition between the H+ and TCH3 +. As the increase of pH values, the adsorption capability is rising until the maximum at pH 8. The most likely reason is that the nickel atoms on the surface of NiFeC bond to the deprotonated C1C3 tricarbonyl methane moiety and the C10C12 phenolic diketone moiety. In addition, the hydroxyl moieties on the surfaces of NiFeC can conduct cation exchange with C4 dimethylammonium group of TC and undertake an important driving force during the adsorption process [35]. Moreover, we can also deduce an electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged tetracycline species like TCH−/TC2− and the positively charged NiFeC surfaces, which enhances the TC adsorption. Although previous work indicates the nickel-tetracycline is stable in the condition of neutral pH [36], our results show the change of NiFeC surface charge and the increase of electrostatic repulsion make a significant low adsorption capability of NiFeC when the pH values exceed 9.69. It is note that Ni2+ is a toxic heavy metal, we have measured the residual Ni2+ and Fe3+ at different pH values after adsorption (Fig. S6). In Fig. S6, the concentration of Ni2+ and Fe3+ are below the drinking water standard recommended by the WHO in our experimental pH range, indicating that NiFeC keeps stable during the whole adsorption process, which is suitable for water purification processes.

The contrast of the NiFe2O4 and NiFeC nanoparticles adsorption ability in the same experimental condition was displayed in Fig.3b. From Fig.3b, the NiFeC nanocomposites adsorption capacity of TC is 530.83mgg−1, which is much more than NiFe2O4 (262.93mgg−1). According to the literature, we can know that tetracycline has multiple polar functional groups such as phenol, ketone, and amino, which can form strong inner-sphere complexes with the exposed nickel ions on the surface of NiFe2O4 [37]. Moreover, it is necessary to note the outstanding adsorption ability of NiFeC nanocomposites is partly attributed to the existence of CDs on the surface of NiFeC, which provides abundant hydroxyl groups for TC removal.

To investigate the adsorption equilibrium time and the kinetic interaction between adsorbate and adsorbent, experiment were assessed at 25°C with initial concentration of 100mgL−1, pH=8. As shown in Fig.4 a, the adsorption capacities increase rapidly in the first 8h, and achieve the adsorption process equilibrium after 24h. The rapid adsorption rate of TC at the early state could be attributed to the abundant adsorption sites on the adsorbents surface. Based on those data, three kinetics models, including pseudo-first-order model, pseudo-second-order kinetics model and intra-particle diffusion model are used to simulate adsorption kinetics of TC on the surface of NiFeC. The pseudo-first-order kinetic model and pseudo-second-order kinetics model are defined as following Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively.
(3)
q
t
=
q
e
× ( 1 -
e
-
k
1
t
)
(4)
q
t
=
q
e
2
K
2
t
1 +
q
e
K
2
t
where K1 and K2 are the equilibrium rate constant of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order equation, respectively. qe and qt represent the adsorption capacity of TC at equilibrium and time t, respectively. Eqs. (5) and (6) represent the linear deformation of the above two kinetics formulas as follows:
(5)
ln
(
q
e
- q ) = ln
q
e
-
k
1
t
(6)
t
q
t
=
1
k
2
q
e
2
+
t
q
e

The k1, k2, and qe can be calculated from the equation and the values are summarized in Table 1 . From the Figs.4a, S7 and Table 1, the pseudo-second-order model (Fig.4a) possesses higher correlation coefficients (R2 >0.99) than pseudo-first-order model (Fig. S7), and indicates this kinetic model is better fitted to the data, suggesting that the chemisorption in the TC adsorption process may be the rate limiting step [15,32].

For further explanation, the intra-particle diffusion model was applied to investigate the diffusion mechanism in TC adsorption process (Fig.4b). The intra-particle diffusion model equation is presented in Eq. (7)
(7)
q
t
=
k
p
t
1 / 2
+ C
Here, kp and C represent the diffusion rate constant of intra-particle and the thickness of boundary layer, respectively. The related parameters kp, C, and R2 are calculated and listed in Table 1. A plot of qt versus t1/2 in Fig.4b shows the multi-linearity relationship can be divided into two stages. The first stage represents the instantaneous external mass transfer of TC from aqueous solution to the adsorbent. The second stage is attributed to the rate-limiting by intraparticle diffusion of TC. It should be pointed out that the intraparticle diffusion is not the dominant rate-controlling step because the plot does not pass through the origin [32].

Adsorption isotherms can describe surface properties and the affinity of adsorbents, thus the experimental data were simulated by different isothermal models to evaluate the adsorption performance of the NiFeC. In this study, the adsorption isotherms of TC (25–100mgL−1) on NiFeC were studied at 303K, 313K and 323K for 36h. The experimental data are analyzed by the two kinds of isotherm models, including Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models (Fig.4c and e), and the formulas of the two models are displayed as Eqs. (8) and (9), respectively.
(8)
q
e
=
K
L
q
max
C
e
1 +
K
L
C
e
(9)
q
e
=
K
f
C
e
1 / n
Here, the qe (mgg−1) and Ce are the adsorption capacity of TC and the concentration of TC at equilibrium, respectively. The parameter qmax (mgg−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity of Langmuir isotherm. KL is the Langmuir adsorption constants that relates to the affinity of adsorption rate and energy of adsorption. Langmuir isotherm assumes that monolayer adsorption occurred in the adsorbed molecules. On the contrary, Freundlich isotherm is upon the assumption of multilayer adsorption on a heterogeneous adsorbents surface. The Freundlich isotherm parameters Ce and qe are as defined above. Additionally, Kf and n represent the Freundlich isotherm constant and Freundlich linearity index, respectively. Table 2 enumerates these two adsorption isotherm fitted parameters and correlation coefficients (R2).

Compared to the correlation coefficients in Table 2 of the two models, it can be concluded that the experimental data is better fitted to the Langmuir isotherm with the R2 values near 1.0. It demonstrates that the adsorption of TC on the surfaces of NiFeC occurs homogeneous via monolayer adsorption [38]. In Fig.4c and e, it is observed that both the two isotherm models of adsorption capacities are enlarged with the increase of temperature, implying that the higher temperature enhances the diffusion rate and speed of adsorption. The maximum adsorption capacity obtained from Langmuir isotherm is 591.72mgg−1, which achieved higher than other previously reported adsorbents such as carbon-based and 1D nanofibers in Table 3 , indicating that the great potential of NiFeC nanocomposites. Moreover, the tetracycline removal capacities of NiFeC were compared with traditional adsorbents (activated carbon, zeolite, etc.) under the same experimental condition (Fig. S8). In Fig. S8, the NiFeC adsorption capacities are much higher than that obtained with traditional adsorbents, indicating the NiFeC is a more suitable adsorbent for the TC removal.

On account of the various salts always co-exists in environmental water, the effect of the ionic strength of four background cations on adsorption capacities was investigated to verify the possibility competition between TC and commonly cations (Figs. 5 and S9). As shown in Fig. 5, TC adsorption efficiency slightly decreased with the increase of the two salts (Na+, K+) concentrations. For the other two cation electrolytes of Mg2+ and Ca2+, the salts concentrations do not generate a statistically significant change in the adsorption of TC (Fig. S9). In such a pH range, the TCH2 and TCH- are the primary species and own the complexing capabilities with Na+ and K+, which possess relatively small hydrated radius [38]. Moreover, the capable of the formation of surface complexing exhibits adsorption-enhancement effect of Na+ and K+ on TC adsorption. Furthermore, the adsorption can develop two different complexes, including weakly bonding of outer-sphere and strongly bonding of inner-sphere complexes [48]. As for Mg2+ and Ca2+, the relatively big hydrated radius inhibit the increase of TC removal efficiency with a rising ionic strength, yet, the influence of overall adsorption is slight after adding the two salts.

To avoid the possibility of the nanomaterials secondary pollution to the environment and assess practical reusability, the regeneration study is performed and the desorption agent is chosen as 0.1M NaOH solution, the adsorption–desorption cycles are executed with five times [49]. The results shown in Fig. S10 illuminate that the overall removal efficiency achieves above 90% after recycling five times. In consequence, the simple synthetic method, high adsorption capacity and excellent reusability of NiFeC certify its great potential in the residential wastewater treatment systems.

To make a further inquiry interaction mechanism of TC with NiFeC adsorbents, FT-IR, TGA as well as XPS analyses of NiFeC before and after TC adsorption are performed. The FT-IR spectra of the NiFeC before and after TC adsorption are display in Fig. 6 , the pure NiFeC functional groups vibration bands of hydroxyl group and metal oxide bonds at 3422cm−1, 606cm−1 are found. The fingerprint peaks of TC range from 1600 to 750cm−1 emerge on the FT-IR spectra of NiFeC after adsorption, which indicate that the TC has been effectively adsorbed on NiFeC [13]. Moreover, after the adsorption of TC, the hydroxyl group shift to 3415cm−1 and metal oxide bonds peaks convert into 592cm−1, possibly ascribing to the cation exchange of hydroxyl group with deprotonated moiety of TC as well as strong surface complexation of adsorbent [50]. Fig. S13 shows TGA of the samples, the carbonization and the decomposition of TC contributes to the obviously earlier weight loss of TC-loaded NiFeC than pure NiFeC, which further illustrate the TC adsorbed NiFeC.

For further demonstration, XPS analysis was carried out to characterize the chemical composition of NiFeC before and after TC adsorption. Fig. S11 shows the high resolution XPS spectra of Ni 2p and Fe 2p of the samples, illustrating that the precursors are successfully transformed into crystalline NiFe2O4 nanoparticles [20]. Figs.7 a and S12 displays the O 1s and C 1s spectra of the samples. In detail, pure NiFeC nanocomposites are deconvoluted into three peaks, which are assigned to O in CO (523.36eV), HO (531.13eV), and NiO/FeO (529.95eV), respectively [1,49,51]. The contents of oxygen containing groups on the NiFeC before and after TC adsorption were listed in Table 4 . Note that the highly hydroxyl group contents ascribing to the CDs confirms the abundant HO groups on the surface of NiFeC. What’s more, the minor increase on the relative content of NiO and FeO after adsorption confirm that the adsorption mechanism could be the surface complexation of the Ni2+ or Fe3+ on the surface of NiFeC, which not only bind to the C1C3 tricarbonyl methane moiety of TC, but also to the C10C12 phenolic diketone moiety of TC as above-mentioned [35]. The sharply decreased trend in the relative content of HO can be observed after adsorption, verifying that the cation exchange between the hydroxyl moieties (OH2+) on the NiFeC surfaces and C4 dimethylammonium group of TC acts as an important driving force during the adsorption process [52,53]. Besides, we measured the point of zero charge of the NiFeC and obtained the pHpzc value of NiFeC (8.7). The results show that the NiFeC possesses a net positive charge under our experimental conditions (pH=8). Hence, we can also deduce an electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged tetracycline species like TCH−/TC2− and the positively charged NiFeC surfaces, which enhances the TC adsorption. Moreover, the increase of the relative content of CO after adsorption manifest a large amount of TC, which possess abundant carbon-oxygen bonds, are adsorbed on the NiFeC. The conclusions can also illustrate in the C 1s XPS spectrum of the samples in Fig. S12 and Table S2, the relative contents of CC (284.77eV) increase after adsorption. It is proposed that chemisorption mainly follows the adsorption process, which is corresponding to the kinetic analysis. Based on the above analysis results, we can confirm that cation exchange contributes greatly to the adsorption of TC on NiFeC because of the abundant hydroxyl moieties (OH2+) on the NiFeC surface, the electrostatic attraction and surface complexation enhanced the TC adsorbed on NiFeC. Scheme 1 illustrates the overall adsorption mechanism of TC on NiFeC adsorbent.

In summary, we reported a one-pot solvothermal method for the rapid synthesis CD-modified NiFe2O4. The merits of abundant surface functionalities and high saturation magnetization value of NiFeC lead to excellent adsorption behavior toward TC and easy separation from solution. Batch adsorption experiments indicated the excellent TC adsorption capacities of NiFeC achieved 591.72mgg−1 at equilibrium pH of 8, and the adsorption process could be well ascribed to the pseudo-second-order kinetics model. In addition, the dominant mechanisms for TC adsorption on NiFeC exhibiting outstanding adsorption property was that surface complexation, cation exchange and electrostatic attraction. This study is highlighted by its simple preparation procedures, high adsorption performance, good regenerability of the adsorbents, which is more suitable for mass-produced and hold great potential in effluent treatment and others water purification process.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financed by Grants from the New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-13-0483), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31371813, No. 31201357), the Shaanxi Provincial Research Fund (2014KJXX-42, 2014K02-13-03, 2014K13-10), Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University of China (2014YB093, 2452015257).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2016.10.116.
